findings.title: emergencyWithdraw() Drains All Tokens Including Collateral and Flash Loan Repayments — Complete Rug
findings.description: emergencyWithdraw() transfers the ENTIRE token balance, which includes: (1) user deposits tracked in `deposits[]`, (2) user collateral tracked in `collateral[]`, (3) accrued flash loan fees. After this call, `totalDeposits` and `totalBorrowed` still retain their old values while the vault holds zero tokens. Every subsequent `withdraw()`, `removeCollateral()`, or `borrow()` call will revert on token.transfer(), permanently locking all user accounting. The contract provides no mechanism to re-initialize balances or pause deposits post-emergency.
findings.recommendation: Add same-block protection (compare `block.number` at entry/exit). Use snapshot-based governance voting. Add timelock delays on sensitive operations.

findings.title: Liquidation Transfers Collateral Without Repaying totalBorrowed — Protocol Accounting Permanently Broken
findings.description: In `liquidate()`, `borrowed[user]` is set to 0 and `collateral[user]` is set to 0, but `totalBorrowed` is NEVER decremented. After liquidation, `totalBorrowed` is permanently inflated. Since `borrow()` checks `totalDeposits - totalBorrowed >= amount`, this means available liquidity as seen by the contract permanently shrinks for every liquidation. Eventually `totalBorrowed` can exceed `totalDeposits`, making `totalDeposits - totalBorrowed` underflow (reverts due to Solidity 0.8.x checked arithmetic), permanently DOSing all future borrows.
findings.message: Protocol DOS, permanently locked funds, incorrect solvency accounting

findings.title: Unprotected Oracle Price Manipulation — Anyone Can Set Price
findings.description: The ManipulableOracle.setPrice() function has no access control whatsoever. Any externally-owned account or contract can call it to set the price of any token to any arbitrary value. Since FlashLoanVault uses this oracle for all collateral valuations (calculateCollateralValue, borrow, liquidate, removeCollateral), an attacker can trivially inflate their own collateral value to borrow everything, or deflate another user's collateral value to liquidate them unfairly.
findings.message: Complete drain of all vault funds. An attacker can: (1) inflate token price to max uint256, add minimal collateral, borrow all available funds; (2) deflate another user's collateral price to near-zero to trigger unjust liquidations and steal their collateral.
findings.recommendation: Add access control to setPrice: require(msg.sender == owner, 'Not owner'). Better yet, replace ManipulableOracle with a TWAP-based oracle (e.g., Uniswap V3 TWAP, Chainlink) that cannot be manipulated in a single transaction.
exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker inspects the contract ABI and identifies a privileged function lacking an access modifier.
2. Attacker calls the unprotected function (e.g. setOwner, mint, pause) directly from any EOA.
3. Without an onlyOwner or role check, the call succeeds immediately.
4. Attacker takes ownership, mints unlimited tokens, or pauses the protocol at will.
5. Legitimate users lose access or funds; attacker drains or disables the contract.

findings.title: Flash Loan Callback Result Not Checked — Attacker Can Skip Repayment Logic
findings.description: In flashLoan(), the external call `(bool success, ) = receiver.call(data)` does not check `success`. This means if the callback reverts or fails, execution continues. While the final balance check `require(balanceAfter >= balanceBefore + fee)` would catch missing repayment, the unchecked call means any logic the receiver was supposed to execute (e.g., repayment approval, internal accounting) can silently fail. More critically, since the boolean lock is set before the external call and reset after, a reentrancy via a different code path (not flashLoan itself) is possible during the callback window.
findings.message: Silent failure of receiver callback. The vault continues to enforce repayment via balance check, but any side effects of a failed callback (e.g., state changes the receiver was supposed to make) are silently ignored, potentially leaving the protocol in an inconsistent state.
findings.recommendation: Require the callback to succeed: `require(success, 'Callback failed')`. Also implement OpenZeppelin's ReentrancyGuard (nonReentrant modifier) instead of a manual boolean lock to prevent reentrancy through other functions during the flash loan callback.
findings.exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker constructs a receiver that does something sensitive before repayment. 2. The callback reverts mid-execution. 3. flashLoan() continues execution, the balance check passes if tokens were already transferred back by some other means. 4. The receiver's partial state changes may have taken effect before the revert, depending on the call context.

findings.title: Reentrancy During Flash Loan via borrow() — Borrow During Active Flash Loan
findings.description: The flashLoanLock boolean only prevents re-entry into flashLoan() itself. During the flash loan callback, the receiver can call borrow(), deposit(), withdraw(), or any other vault function freely. Specifically, an attacker with flash-loaned funds can: manipulate the oracle (since oracle has no access control), then call borrow() to extract additional funds while collateral value is artificially inflated. The borrow() function explicitly has no check for flashLoanLock state.
findings.message: Attacker can borrow far more than their legitimate collateral allows by combining flash loan + oracle manipulation + borrow in a single transaction, draining vault liquidity.
findings.recommendation: Apply the nonReentrant modifier to ALL state-changing functions (deposit, withdraw, borrow, repay, addCollateral, removeCollateral, liquidate). Use OpenZeppelin's ReentrancyGuard. Additionally, borrow() should explicitly check `require(!flashLoanLock, 'Flash loan in progress')`.
exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker deploys a malicious contract with a fallback/receive function that calls back into the victim.
2. Attacker calls the victim's withdraw() function with a valid balance.
3. Victim sends ETH to attacker — attacker's fallback immediately calls withdraw() again before state is updated.
4. Re-entry loop drains the contract balance in a single transaction (no per-call state update blocks it).
5. Attack ends when gas runs out or balance is exhausted; attacker keeps all drained ETH.

findings.title: emergencyWithdraw() Drains All User Funds With No Timelock — Rug Pull
findings.description: The emergencyWithdraw() function transfers the entire token balance of the vault to the owner address with no timelock, no governance delay, no multi-sig requirement. This includes all user deposits and collateral. The owner can call this at any time to instantly drain 100% of user funds. This is a complete centralization risk and rug pull vector.
findings.message: Owner can drain 100% of all user deposits and collateral instantly. All users lose their funds with no warning or recourse.
findings.recommendation: Implement a timelock (minimum 48-72 hours) before emergency withdrawal executes. Use a multi-sig owner. Alternatively, only allow withdrawal of excess funds (balance - totalDeposits - totalCollateral). Emit events and allow users to withdraw during the timelock period.
findings.exploit_scenario: 1. Users deposit funds into vault over time. 2. Owner calls emergencyWithdraw(). 3. All token.balanceOf(address(this)) transferred to owner. 4. Users cannot withdraw their deposits or recover collateral.

findings.title: Spot Price Oracle Used for All Financial Calculations — Flash Loan Price Manipulation
findings.description: All collateral valuations use a spot price from a single oracle (getTokenPrice -> oracle.getPrice) with no TWAP protection and no price bounds checking. Combined with the fact that the oracle has no access control (anyone can set prices), this means within a single transaction an attacker can manipulate the price, perform profitable actions, and restore the price — all atomically. Even if the oracle were properly permissioned, using spot prices allows flash-loan-based price manipulation attacks.
findings.message: Complete draining of vault funds via manipulated collateral valuations for borrowing or to trigger/prevent liquidations.
findings.recommendation: Use Chainlink price feeds with staleness checks, or Uniswap V3 TWAP with a minimum observation window (e.g., 30 minutes). Add price deviation checks (revert if price moves >20% from last observation). Never use a mutable spot price oracle for critical financial calculations.
findings.exploit_scenario: See FlashLoanAttacker.attack() and executeAttack() in the contract — the attack vector is literally implemented in the codebase: 1) flash loan full vault balance, 2) set oracle price to 1, 3) liquidate all positions for free collateral, 4) set price back, 5) repay flash loan.

findings.title: Flash Loan + Borrow Cross-Function Reentrancy via flashLoanLock Bypass
findings.description: During a flash loan, the `flashLoanLock` is set to true but `borrow()` does NOT check `flashLoanLock`. An attacker receiving tokens in a flash loan can call `borrow()` within the same transaction. Because the vault's token balance is temporarily reduced (tokens sent out), `totalDeposits - totalBorrowed` still reflects pre-loan state, allowing borrowing against collateral whose value may be transiently inflated by oracle manipulation. The attacker borrows real funds, then repays the flash loan, keeping the borrowed tokens — effectively extracting value.
findings.recommendation: Apply Checks-Effects-Interactions pattern: update state BEFORE external calls. Use OpenZeppelin's ReentrancyGuard (`nonReentrant` modifier).
exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker deploys a malicious contract with a fallback/receive function that calls back into the victim.
2. Attacker calls the victim's withdraw() function with a valid balance.
3. Victim sends ETH to attacker — attacker's fallback immediately calls withdraw() again before state is updated.
4. Re-entry loop drains the contract balance in a single transaction (no per-call state update blocks it).
5. Attack ends when gas runs out or balance is exhausted; attacker keeps all drained ETH.

findings.title: Zero-Price Oracle Returns Zero Collateral Value — All Positions Instantly Liquidatable
findings.description: If `oracle.getPrice()` returns 0 (initial state of ManipulableOracle before any price is set, or after a malicious setPrice(token, 0) call), `calculateCollateralValue()` returns 0 for all users. Then in `liquidate()`, the check `collateralValue * 100 < borrowedValue * COLLATERAL_RATIO` evaluates as `0 < borrowedValue * 150`, which is TRUE for any non-zero borrower. Every user with outstanding borrows can be immediately liquidated by any caller. The liquidator receives `collateral[user]` tokens for free (no payment required), extracting all collateral. Combined with the ManipulableOracle having no access control on setPrice(), this is a complete protocol wipe attack.
findings.recommendation: Use time-weighted average prices (TWAP) instead of spot prices. Add staleness checks on Chainlink `latestRoundData()`. Never use `getReserves()` for pricing.
exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker takes a flash loan of a large amount of the quote token from a lending protocol.
2. Attacker dumps the borrowed token into the DEX pool, crashing the spot price.
3. Victim contract reads the manipulated spot price via getReserves() or similar — treats it as market price.
4. Attacker exploits the mispriced collateral: borrows against inflated collateral or liquidates positions unfairly.
5. Attacker swaps back to restore the price, repays the flash loan, and pockets the arbitrage profit.
6. Entire attack is atomic — completes within a single transaction with no capital at risk.

findings.message: Function assigns ownership but may not be the constructor — in Solidity <0.4.22, constructors were functions with the contract name. A typo means anyone can call it and become owner.
findings.description: Function assigns ownership but may not be the constructor — in Solidity <0.4.22, constructors were functions with the contract name. A typo means anyone can call it and become owner.
findings.recommendation: Function assigns ownership but may not be the constructor — in Solidity <0.4.22, constructors were functions with the contract name. A typo means anyone can call it and become owner.

findings.message: Reentrancy in FlashLoanVault.flashLoan(uint256,address,bytes) (../data/audit/FlashLoanVault.sol#59-83):
findings.description:
Reentrancy in [FlashLoanVault.flashLoan(uint256,address,bytes)](../data/audit/FlashLoanVault.sol#L59-L83):
	External call allowing reentrancy:
	- [require(bool,string)(token.transfer(receiver,amount),Transfer failed)](../data/audit/FlashLoanVault.sol#L69)
	Balance read before the call:
	- [balanceBefore = token.balanceOf(address(this))](../data/audit/FlashLoanVault.sol#L63)
	Possible stale balance used after the call in a condition:
	- [require(bool,string)(balanceAfter >= balanceBefore + fee,Flash loan not repaid)](../data/audit/FlashLoanVault.sol#L79)
		- stale variable `balanceBefore`
findings.recommendation: Apply Checks-Effects-Interactions pattern: update state BEFORE external calls. Use OpenZeppelin's ReentrancyGuard (`nonReentrant` modifier).
exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker deploys a malicious contract with a fallback/receive function that calls back into the victim.
2. Attacker calls the victim's withdraw() function with a valid balance.
3. Victim sends ETH to attacker — attacker's fallback immediately calls withdraw() again before state is updated.
4. Re-entry loop drains the contract balance in a single transaction (no per-call state update blocks it).
5. Attack ends when gas runs out or balance is exhausted; attacker keeps all drained ETH.

findings.message: Contract locks Ether without a withdraw function.
findings.description: It appears that the contract includes a payable function to accept Ether but lacks a corresponding function to withdraw it, which leads to the Ether being locked in the contract. To resolve this issue, please implement a public or external function that allows for the withdrawal of Ether from the contract.
findings.recommendation: Review and fix the issue according to best practices

findings.title: Liquidation Transfers Collateral Without Accounting for Borrowed Repayment
findings.description: The liquidate() function sets collateral[user] = 0 and borrowed[user] = 0, then transfers collateral to the liquidator — but never updates totalBorrowed or totalDeposits. This means totalBorrowed remains inflated after liquidation, permanently reducing the liquidity available for new borrows (totalDeposits - totalBorrowed check in borrow()). Over time, repeated liquidations cause totalBorrowed to be permanently overstated relative to actual outstanding debt, eventually making it impossible to borrow anything.
findings.message: Protocol accounting becomes permanently corrupted after any liquidation. totalBorrowed is never decremented, making the vault increasingly unusable for legitimate borrowers. Additionally, the liquidator receives collateral tokens that were supposedly backing the loan, but no actual loan repayment occurs — the protocol takes a loss equal to the borrowed amount.
findings.recommendation: In liquidate(), decrement totalBorrowed by the liquidated borrow amount: `totalBorrowed -= liquidationAmount` (where liquidationAmount refers to borrowed[user] before zeroing). Also require the liquidator to repay the debt: use transferFrom to pull the borrowed amount from the liquidator before transferring collateral.
findings.exploit_scenario: 1. User borrows 1000 tokens (totalBorrowed = 1000). 2. Oracle price drops, user becomes liquidatable. 3. Liquidator calls liquidate(user). 4. collateral[user] = 0, borrowed[user] = 0, but totalBorrowed remains 1000. 5. Repeat for multiple users. 6. totalBorrowed accumulates to massive value. 7. borrow() requires totalDeposits - totalBorrowed >= amount, which eventually underflows or makes new borrows impossible.

findings.message: External send/transfer inside require — if recipient reverts, entire function is blocked (DoS). Use pull pattern instead.
findings.description: External send/transfer inside require — if recipient reverts, entire function is blocked (DoS). Use pull pattern instead.
findings.recommendation: External send/transfer inside require — if recipient reverts, entire function is blocked (DoS). Use pull pattern instead.

findings.message: Event is missing `indexed` fields
findings.description: Index event fields make the field more quickly accessible to off-chain tools that parse events. However, note that each index field costs extra gas during emission, so it's not necessarily best to index the maximum allowed per event (three fields). Each event should use three indexed fields if there are three or more fields, and gas usage is not particularly of concern for the events in question. If there are fewer than three fields, all of the fields should be indexed.
findings.recommendation: Review and fix the issue according to best practices

findings.message: Unsafe ERC20 Operations should not be used
findings.description: ERC20 functions may not behave as expected. For example: return values are not always meaningful. It is recommended to use OpenZeppelin's SafeERC20 library.
findings.recommendation: Review and fix the issue according to best practices

findings.message: Low-level call without explicit success check — use require(success) or SafeERC20.
findings.description: Low-level call without explicit success check — use require(success) or SafeERC20.
findings.recommendation: Low-level call without explicit success check — use require(success) or SafeERC20.
exploit_scenario: 1. Contract calls an external address using .call() or .send() but does not check the boolean return value.
2. The external call silently fails (e.g. recipient is a contract that reverts, or gas stipend is exceeded).
3. Because the return value is ignored, the contract continues execution and marks the operation as successful.
4. Tokens or ETH are never delivered, but the contract's internal state (balances, flags) is updated as if they were.
5. Attacker exploits the accounting discrepancy to double-spend or claim funds they should not have.

findings.message: Contract locking ether found:

	But does not have a function to withdraw the ether
findings.recommendation: Provide a withdrawal function or ensure contract is payable

findings.message: Define and use `constant` variables instead of using literals
findings.description: If the same constant literal value is used multiple times, create a constant state variable and reference it throughout the contract.
findings.recommendation: Review and fix the issue according to best practices

findings.message: Approval pattern susceptible to front-running race condition. Consider increaseAllowance/decreaseAllowance.
findings.description: Approval pattern susceptible to front-running race condition. Consider increaseAllowance/decreaseAllowance.
findings.recommendation: Approval pattern susceptible to front-running race condition. Consider increaseAllowance/decreaseAllowance.
exploit_scenario: 1. Victim calls token.approve(spender, 100) to change an existing approval from 50 to 100.
2. Attacker monitors the mempool and sees the approve transaction before it is mined.
3. Attacker front-runs by calling transferFrom(victim, attacker, 50) while the old allowance of 50 is still active.
4. After the victim's approve is mined, attacker calls transferFrom again using the new allowance of 100.
5. Attacker steals 150 tokens even though the victim intended to grant only 100.
6. Fix: use increaseAllowance/decreaseAllowance which are atomic and race-free.

findings.message: transfer()/send() forwards only 2300 gas — will fail with contract recipients after EIP-1884. Use call{value:}() with reentrancy guard.
findings.description: transfer()/send() forwards only 2300 gas — will fail with contract recipients after EIP-1884. Use call{value:}() with reentrancy guard.
findings.recommendation: transfer()/send() forwards only 2300 gas — will fail with contract recipients after EIP-1884. Use call{value:}() with reentrancy guard.

findings.message: Reentrancy in FlashLoanVault.repay(uint256) (../data/audit/FlashLoanVault.sol#157-165):
findings.description:
Reentrancy in [FlashLoanVault.repay(uint256)](../data/audit/FlashLoanVault.sol#L157-L165):
	External calls:
	- [require(bool,string)(token.transferFrom(msg.sender,address(this),amount),Transfer failed)](../data/audit/FlashLoanVault.sol#L158)
	State variables written after the call(s):
	- [borrowed[msg.sender] -= amount](../data/audit/FlashLoanVault.sol#L161)
	- [totalBorrowed -= amount](../data/audit/FlashLoanVault.sol#L162)
findings.recommendation: Apply Checks-Effects-Interactions pattern: update state BEFORE external calls. Use OpenZeppelin's ReentrancyGuard (`nonReentrant` modifier).
exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker deploys a malicious contract with a fallback/receive function that calls back into the victim.
2. Attacker calls the victim's withdraw() function with a valid balance.
3. Victim sends ETH to attacker — attacker's fallback immediately calls withdraw() again before state is updated.
4. Re-entry loop drains the contract balance in a single transaction (no per-call state update blocks it).
5. Attack ends when gas runs out or balance is exhausted; attacker keeps all drained ETH.

findings.recommendation: Add same-block protection (compare `block.number` at entry/exit). Use snapshot-based governance voting. Add timelock delays on sensitive operations.

findings.title: Liquidation Transfers Collateral Without Repaying totalBorrowed — Protocol Accounting Permanently Broken
findings.description: In `liquidate()`, `borrowed[user]` is set to 0 and `collateral[user]` is set to 0, but `totalBorrowed` is NEVER decremented. After liquidation, `totalBorrowed` is permanently inflated. Since `borrow()` checks `totalDeposits - totalBorrowed >= amount`, this means available liquidity as seen by the contract permanently shrinks for every liquidation. Eventually `totalBorrowed` can exceed `totalDeposits`, making `totalDeposits - totalBorrowed` underflow (reverts due to Solidity 0.8.x checked arithmetic), permanently DOSing all future borrows.
findings.message: Protocol DOS, permanently locked funds, incorrect solvency accounting

findings.title: Unprotected Oracle Price Manipulation — Anyone Can Set Price
findings.description: The ManipulableOracle.setPrice() function has no access control whatsoever. Any externally-owned account or contract can call it to set the price of any token to any arbitrary value. Since FlashLoanVault uses this oracle for all collateral valuations (calculateCollateralValue, borrow, liquidate, removeCollateral), an attacker can trivially inflate their own collateral value to borrow everything, or deflate another user's collateral value to liquidate them unfairly.
findings.message: Complete drain of all vault funds. An attacker can: (1) inflate token price to max uint256, add minimal collateral, borrow all available funds; (2) deflate another user's collateral price to near-zero to trigger unjust liquidations and steal their collateral.
findings.recommendation: Add access control to setPrice: require(msg.sender == owner, 'Not owner'). Better yet, replace ManipulableOracle with a TWAP-based oracle (e.g., Uniswap V3 TWAP, Chainlink) that cannot be manipulated in a single transaction.
exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker inspects the contract ABI and identifies a privileged function lacking an access modifier.
2. Attacker calls the unprotected function (e.g. setOwner, mint, pause) directly from any EOA.
3. Without an onlyOwner or role check, the call succeeds immediately.
4. Attacker takes ownership, mints unlimited tokens, or pauses the protocol at will.
5. Legitimate users lose access or funds; attacker drains or disables the contract.

findings.title: Flash Loan Callback Result Not Checked — Attacker Can Skip Repayment Logic
findings.description: In flashLoan(), the external call `(bool success, ) = receiver.call(data)` does not check `success`. This means if the callback reverts or fails, execution continues. While the final balance check `require(balanceAfter >= balanceBefore + fee)` would catch missing repayment, the unchecked call means any logic the receiver was supposed to execute (e.g., repayment approval, internal accounting) can silently fail. More critically, since the boolean lock is set before the external call and reset after, a reentrancy via a different code path (not flashLoan itself) is possible during the callback window.
findings.message: Silent failure of receiver callback. The vault continues to enforce repayment via balance check, but any side effects of a failed callback (e.g., state changes the receiver was supposed to make) are silently ignored, potentially leaving the protocol in an inconsistent state.
findings.recommendation: Require the callback to succeed: `require(success, 'Callback failed')`. Also implement OpenZeppelin's ReentrancyGuard (nonReentrant modifier) instead of a manual boolean lock to prevent reentrancy through other functions during the flash loan callback.
findings.exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker constructs a receiver that does something sensitive before repayment. 2. The callback reverts mid-execution. 3. flashLoan() continues execution, the balance check passes if tokens were already transferred back by some other means. 4. The receiver's partial state changes may have taken effect before the revert, depending on the call context.

findings.title: Reentrancy During Flash Loan via borrow() — Borrow During Active Flash Loan
findings.description: The flashLoanLock boolean only prevents re-entry into flashLoan() itself. During the flash loan callback, the receiver can call borrow(), deposit(), withdraw(), or any other vault function freely. Specifically, an attacker with flash-loaned funds can: manipulate the oracle (since oracle has no access control), then call borrow() to extract additional funds while collateral value is artificially inflated. The borrow() function explicitly has no check for flashLoanLock state.
findings.message: Attacker can borrow far more than their legitimate collateral allows by combining flash loan + oracle manipulation + borrow in a single transaction, draining vault liquidity.
findings.recommendation: Apply the nonReentrant modifier to ALL state-changing functions (deposit, withdraw, borrow, repay, addCollateral, removeCollateral, liquidate). Use OpenZeppelin's ReentrancyGuard. Additionally, borrow() should explicitly check `require(!flashLoanLock, 'Flash loan in progress')`.
exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker deploys a malicious contract with a fallback/receive function that calls back into the victim.
2. Attacker calls the victim's withdraw() function with a valid balance.
3. Victim sends ETH to attacker — attacker's fallback immediately calls withdraw() again before state is updated.
4. Re-entry loop drains the contract balance in a single transaction (no per-call state update blocks it).
5. Attack ends when gas runs out or balance is exhausted; attacker keeps all drained ETH.

findings.title: emergencyWithdraw() Drains All User Funds With No Timelock — Rug Pull
findings.description: The emergencyWithdraw() function transfers the entire token balance of the vault to the owner address with no timelock, no governance delay, no multi-sig requirement. This includes all user deposits and collateral. The owner can call this at any time to instantly drain 100% of user funds. This is a complete centralization risk and rug pull vector.
findings.message: Owner can drain 100% of all user deposits and collateral instantly. All users lose their funds with no warning or recourse.
findings.recommendation: Implement a timelock (minimum 48-72 hours) before emergency withdrawal executes. Use a multi-sig owner. Alternatively, only allow withdrawal of excess funds (balance - totalDeposits - totalCollateral). Emit events and allow users to withdraw during the timelock period.
findings.exploit_scenario: 1. Users deposit funds into vault over time. 2. Owner calls emergencyWithdraw(). 3. All token.balanceOf(address(this)) transferred to owner. 4. Users cannot withdraw their deposits or recover collateral.

findings.title: Spot Price Oracle Used for All Financial Calculations — Flash Loan Price Manipulation
findings.description: All collateral valuations use a spot price from a single oracle (getTokenPrice -> oracle.getPrice) with no TWAP protection and no price bounds checking. Combined with the fact that the oracle has no access control (anyone can set prices), this means within a single transaction an attacker can manipulate the price, perform profitable actions, and restore the price — all atomically. Even if the oracle were properly permissioned, using spot prices allows flash-loan-based price manipulation attacks.
findings.message: Complete draining of vault funds via manipulated collateral valuations for borrowing or to trigger/prevent liquidations.
findings.recommendation: Use Chainlink price feeds with staleness checks, or Uniswap V3 TWAP with a minimum observation window (e.g., 30 minutes). Add price deviation checks (revert if price moves >20% from last observation). Never use a mutable spot price oracle for critical financial calculations.
findings.exploit_scenario: See FlashLoanAttacker.attack() and executeAttack() in the contract — the attack vector is literally implemented in the codebase: 1) flash loan full vault balance, 2) set oracle price to 1, 3) liquidate all positions for free collateral, 4) set price back, 5) repay flash loan.

findings.title: Flash Loan + Borrow Cross-Function Reentrancy via flashLoanLock Bypass
findings.description: During a flash loan, the `flashLoanLock` is set to true but `borrow()` does NOT check `flashLoanLock`. An attacker receiving tokens in a flash loan can call `borrow()` within the same transaction. Because the vault's token balance is temporarily reduced (tokens sent out), `totalDeposits - totalBorrowed` still reflects pre-loan state, allowing borrowing against collateral whose value may be transiently inflated by oracle manipulation. The attacker borrows real funds, then repays the flash loan, keeping the borrowed tokens — effectively extracting value.
findings.recommendation: Apply Checks-Effects-Interactions pattern: update state BEFORE external calls. Use OpenZeppelin's ReentrancyGuard (`nonReentrant` modifier).
exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker deploys a malicious contract with a fallback/receive function that calls back into the victim.
2. Attacker calls the victim's withdraw() function with a valid balance.
3. Victim sends ETH to attacker — attacker's fallback immediately calls withdraw() again before state is updated.
4. Re-entry loop drains the contract balance in a single transaction (no per-call state update blocks it).
5. Attack ends when gas runs out or balance is exhausted; attacker keeps all drained ETH.

findings.title: Zero-Price Oracle Returns Zero Collateral Value — All Positions Instantly Liquidatable
findings.description: If `oracle.getPrice()` returns 0 (initial state of ManipulableOracle before any price is set, or after a malicious setPrice(token, 0) call), `calculateCollateralValue()` returns 0 for all users. Then in `liquidate()`, the check `collateralValue * 100 < borrowedValue * COLLATERAL_RATIO` evaluates as `0 < borrowedValue * 150`, which is TRUE for any non-zero borrower. Every user with outstanding borrows can be immediately liquidated by any caller. The liquidator receives `collateral[user]` tokens for free (no payment required), extracting all collateral. Combined with the ManipulableOracle having no access control on setPrice(), this is a complete protocol wipe attack.
findings.recommendation: Use time-weighted average prices (TWAP) instead of spot prices. Add staleness checks on Chainlink `latestRoundData()`. Never use `getReserves()` for pricing.
exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker takes a flash loan of a large amount of the quote token from a lending protocol.
2. Attacker dumps the borrowed token into the DEX pool, crashing the spot price.
3. Victim contract reads the manipulated spot price via getReserves() or similar — treats it as market price.
4. Attacker exploits the mispriced collateral: borrows against inflated collateral or liquidates positions unfairly.
5. Attacker swaps back to restore the price, repays the flash loan, and pockets the arbitrage profit.
6. Entire attack is atomic — completes within a single transaction with no capital at risk.

findings.message: Function assigns ownership but may not be the constructor — in Solidity <0.4.22, constructors were functions with the contract name. A typo means anyone can call it and become owner.
findings.description: Function assigns ownership but may not be the constructor — in Solidity <0.4.22, constructors were functions with the contract name. A typo means anyone can call it and become owner.
findings.recommendation: Function assigns ownership but may not be the constructor — in Solidity <0.4.22, constructors were functions with the contract name. A typo means anyone can call it and become owner.

findings.message: Reentrancy in FlashLoanVault.flashLoan(uint256,address,bytes) (../data/audit/FlashLoanVault.sol#59-83):
findings.description:
Reentrancy in [FlashLoanVault.flashLoan(uint256,address,bytes)](../data/audit/FlashLoanVault.sol#L59-L83):
	External call allowing reentrancy:
	- [require(bool,string)(token.transfer(receiver,amount),Transfer failed)](../data/audit/FlashLoanVault.sol#L69)
	Balance read before the call:
	- [balanceBefore = token.balanceOf(address(this))](../data/audit/FlashLoanVault.sol#L63)
	Possible stale balance used after the call in a condition:
	- [require(bool,string)(balanceAfter >= balanceBefore + fee,Flash loan not repaid)](../data/audit/FlashLoanVault.sol#L79)
		- stale variable `balanceBefore`
findings.recommendation: Apply Checks-Effects-Interactions pattern: update state BEFORE external calls. Use OpenZeppelin's ReentrancyGuard (`nonReentrant` modifier).
exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker deploys a malicious contract with a fallback/receive function that calls back into the victim.
2. Attacker calls the victim's withdraw() function with a valid balance.
3. Victim sends ETH to attacker — attacker's fallback immediately calls withdraw() again before state is updated.
4. Re-entry loop drains the contract balance in a single transaction (no per-call state update blocks it).
5. Attack ends when gas runs out or balance is exhausted; attacker keeps all drained ETH.

findings.message: Contract locks Ether without a withdraw function.
findings.description: It appears that the contract includes a payable function to accept Ether but lacks a corresponding function to withdraw it, which leads to the Ether being locked in the contract. To resolve this issue, please implement a public or external function that allows for the withdrawal of Ether from the contract.
findings.recommendation: Review and fix the issue according to best practices

findings.title: Liquidation Transfers Collateral Without Accounting for Borrowed Repayment
findings.description: The liquidate() function sets collateral[user] = 0 and borrowed[user] = 0, then transfers collateral to the liquidator — but never updates totalBorrowed or totalDeposits. This means totalBorrowed remains inflated after liquidation, permanently reducing the liquidity available for new borrows (totalDeposits - totalBorrowed check in borrow()). Over time, repeated liquidations cause totalBorrowed to be permanently overstated relative to actual outstanding debt, eventually making it impossible to borrow anything.
findings.message: Protocol accounting becomes permanently corrupted after any liquidation. totalBorrowed is never decremented, making the vault increasingly unusable for legitimate borrowers. Additionally, the liquidator receives collateral tokens that were supposedly backing the loan, but no actual loan repayment occurs — the protocol takes a loss equal to the borrowed amount.
findings.recommendation: In liquidate(), decrement totalBorrowed by the liquidated borrow amount: `totalBorrowed -= liquidationAmount` (where liquidationAmount refers to borrowed[user] before zeroing). Also require the liquidator to repay the debt: use transferFrom to pull the borrowed amount from the liquidator before transferring collateral.
findings.exploit_scenario: 1. User borrows 1000 tokens (totalBorrowed = 1000). 2. Oracle price drops, user becomes liquidatable. 3. Liquidator calls liquidate(user). 4. collateral[user] = 0, borrowed[user] = 0, but totalBorrowed remains 1000. 5. Repeat for multiple users. 6. totalBorrowed accumulates to massive value. 7. borrow() requires totalDeposits - totalBorrowed >= amount, which eventually underflows or makes new borrows impossible.

findings.message: External send/transfer inside require — if recipient reverts, entire function is blocked (DoS). Use pull pattern instead.
findings.description: External send/transfer inside require — if recipient reverts, entire function is blocked (DoS). Use pull pattern instead.
findings.recommendation: External send/transfer inside require — if recipient reverts, entire function is blocked (DoS). Use pull pattern instead.

findings.message: Event is missing `indexed` fields
findings.description: Index event fields make the field more quickly accessible to off-chain tools that parse events. However, note that each index field costs extra gas during emission, so it's not necessarily best to index the maximum allowed per event (three fields). Each event should use three indexed fields if there are three or more fields, and gas usage is not particularly of concern for the events in question. If there are fewer than three fields, all of the fields should be indexed.
findings.recommendation: Review and fix the issue according to best practices

findings.message: Unsafe ERC20 Operations should not be used
findings.description: ERC20 functions may not behave as expected. For example: return values are not always meaningful. It is recommended to use OpenZeppelin's SafeERC20 library.
findings.recommendation: Review and fix the issue according to best practices

findings.recommendation: Review and fix the issue according to best practices

findings.message: Low-level call without explicit success check — use require(success) or SafeERC20.
findings.description: Low-level call without explicit success check — use require(success) or SafeERC20.
findings.recommendation: Low-level call without explicit success check — use require(success) or SafeERC20.
exploit_scenario: 1. Contract calls an external address using .call() or .send() but does not check the boolean return value.
2. The external call silently fails (e.g. recipient is a contract that reverts, or gas stipend is exceeded).
3. Because the return value is ignored, the contract continues execution and marks the operation as successful.
4. Tokens or ETH are never delivered, but the contract's internal state (balances, flags) is updated as if they were.
5. Attacker exploits the accounting discrepancy to double-spend or claim funds they should not have.

	But does not have a function to withdraw the ether

findings.message: Approval pattern susceptible to front-running race condition. Consider increaseAllowance/decreaseAllowance.
findings.description: Approval pattern susceptible to front-running race condition. Consider increaseAllowance/decreaseAllowance.
findings.recommendation: Approval pattern susceptible to front-running race condition. Consider increaseAllowance/decreaseAllowance.
exploit_scenario: 1. Victim calls token.approve(spender, 100) to change an existing approval from 50 to 100.
2. Attacker monitors the mempool and sees the approve transaction before it is mined.
3. Attacker front-runs by calling transferFrom(victim, attacker, 50) while the old allowance of 50 is still active.
4. After the victim's approve is mined, attacker calls transferFrom again using the new allowance of 100.
5. Attacker steals 150 tokens even though the victim intended to grant only 100.
6. Fix: use increaseAllowance/decreaseAllowance which are atomic and race-free.

findings.message: transfer()/send() forwards only 2300 gas — will fail with contract recipients after EIP-1884. Use call{value:}() with reentrancy guard.
findings.description: transfer()/send() forwards only 2300 gas — will fail with contract recipients after EIP-1884. Use call{value:}() with reentrancy guard.
findings.recommendation: transfer()/send() forwards only 2300 gas — will fail with contract recipients after EIP-1884. Use call{value:}() with reentrancy guard.

findings.message: Reentrancy in FlashLoanVault.repay(uint256) (../data/audit/FlashLoanVault.sol#157-165):
findings.description:
Reentrancy in [FlashLoanVault.repay(uint256)](../data/audit/FlashLoanVault.sol#L157-L165):
	External calls:
	- [require(bool,string)(token.transferFrom(msg.sender,address(this),amount),Transfer failed)](../data/audit/FlashLoanVault.sol#L158)
	State variables written after the call(s):
	- [borrowed[msg.sender] -= amount](../data/audit/FlashLoanVault.sol#L161)
	- [totalBorrowed -= amount](../data/audit/FlashLoanVault.sol#L162)
findings.recommendation: Apply Checks-Effects-Interactions pattern: update state BEFORE external calls. Use OpenZeppelin's ReentrancyGuard (`nonReentrant` modifier).

findings.message: State-changing function without event emission — limits off-chain monitoring and indexing.
findings.description: State-changing function without event emission — limits off-chain monitoring and indexing.
findings.recommendation: State-changing function without event emission — limits off-chain monitoring and indexing.
